meta.title_he: Renovation knowledge base — planning a private home
meta.what_he: A planning guide for common private-home renovations: estimated cost, permit/exemption, duration and considerations. Meant for the planning tool in the renovation tab — not a substitute for a contractor's quote or for advice from an architect/engineer.
meta.entry_shape: { id, name_he, emoji, cost_he, permit_he, duration_he, considerations_he[], suits[] }

note_he: All prices here are estimates only — rough ranges for the 2026 area, not a quote. The actual cost depends on location (in Larkmont and outlying areas, hauling and labor are sometimes more expensive due to the distance from the center), on the finish level, on shifting material prices and on the condition of the existing house. Before committing — get two or three detailed written quotes, and check with an architect/engineer about what requires a permit. There is no single 'right' figure here, only a range for initial planning.
permit_overview_he: Under the local permit-exemption rules, some works are exempt from a building permit subject to conditions (size, location, and sometimes filing an engineer's declaration / a notice to the authority). Interior renovation that does not touch load-bearing walls, the built area or the building envelope — usually exempt. Demolishing a load-bearing wall, adding floor area/a room, changing the facade, and any structural change — require a building permit and engineering design. The reinforced safe room is a separate track under the safe-room standard. An exemption does not waive compliance with the standard or safety responsibility — always verify with the local authority and the relevant professional.

sources_he.what_he: The local permit-exemption rules and their amendments — they specify which works are exempt from a permit and under what conditions (pergola, shed, fence, solar panels, interior changes and more).

The specification for a home's reinforced safe room: concrete wall thickness, a reinforced door and window, ventilation and filtration — a separate approval track under the safe-room standard, including in a renovation/addition.
The body that actually approves building permits. Before any significant work — check with the authority's engineer what requires a permit and what is exempt in the specific area.
Plumbing, waterproofing and electrical standards — wet works and electrical work require a licensed professional (a licensed plumber / a certified electrician).

renovations.cost_he: A wide range by size and finish: a budget kitchen ~$25,000–45,000; mid-range ~$45,000–90,000; luxury/large $90,000–200,000+. Cabinets + countertop alone are usually $1,500–4,000 per linear meter.
renovations.permit_he: Usually exempt from a permit as long as you don't move a load-bearing wall or change the built area. Relocating water/sewage and electrical points — no permit needed, but requires a licensed professional.
renovations.duration_he: 2–5 weeks (a full kitchen including demolition, plumbing, electrical, spot flooring and tiling).
considerations_he: Relocating the water/sewage station is among the costliest and most complex tasks — keeping the wet zone in place saves significantly.
Coordinate the sequence: electrical and plumbing in the wall → plaster/tiling → flooring → cabinets → countertop → final electrical.
A natural-stone/quartz countertop versus laminate changes the price dramatically; measure the openings for the fridge/oven in advance.
Ventilation and a range-hood point, plus planning enough outlets — easy and cheap now, expensive to fix later.

renovations.cost_he: A small basic bathroom ~$18,000–35,000; mid-range/a modern shower ~$35,000–60,000; large/luxury $60,000–120,000+. A guest restroom only ~$8,000–18,000.
renovations.permit_he: Exempt from a permit (interior renovation). But plumbing and waterproofing require a licensed plumber and compliance with the waterproofing and plumbing standard; relocating a toilet/drain is complex.
renovations.duration_he: 2–4 weeks (demolition, piping, waterproofing + drying time, flooring and tiling, fixtures).
considerations_he: A wet area — proper waterproofing (membranes/sealing under the flooring) is the most critical part; a waterproofing failure = expensive damage to the neighbors/the house.
Moving a drain/toilet requires slopes and raising the floor — one of the costly items; better to keep the location.
Allow drying time for the waterproofing and mortar before flooring — time pressure here causes leaks.
Ventilation (a window/fan) is essential against mold; consider floor/towel heating in Larkmont's cold climate.

renovations.cost_he: About $150–450 per m² (labor + material) for standard ceramic/porcelain tile; stone/parquet/marble $400–900+ per m². Including removal and disposal of the old floor — add ~$40–90 per m².
renovations.permit_he: Completely exempt from a permit (interior finish only).
renovations.duration_he: About 3–7 days for an average apartment/floor, plus time for curing and for the dust to settle; furniture removal required.
considerations_he: Tiling over the existing floor is cheaper and faster but raises the floor height — check that doors and entry thresholds still fit.
A full replacement requires demolition that is noisy and dusty; plan alternative living arrangements/zoning off areas.
A large tile looks spacious but requires a level floor and substrate leveling — an added cost.
In a cold climate — consider underfloor heating right now, before the substrate is covered.

renovations.cost_he: An interior non-load-bearing wall (drywall/block): ~$1,500–5,000 including removal and plaster/electrical repairs. A load-bearing wall (opening it + a reinforcing steel/concrete beam): ~$8,000–25,000+ including the structural work and the permit.
renovations.permit_he: An interior non-load-bearing wall — exempt. A load-bearing wall — a building permit + a structural engineer's design are mandatory; do not touch it without approval.
renovations.duration_he: Non-load-bearing wall: 1–3 days + finishing repairs. Load-bearing wall: one to two weeks of work, but the design + permit may take months.
considerations_he: First determine whether the wall bears load — a structural engineer is mandatory; a mistake here endangers the structure.
A wall may contain piping/electrical/water — relocate it before demolition, which adds cost.
Opening up a space requires repairing the floor/ceiling/plaster along the line of the removed wall.
Opening into an open space improves light and the sense of space but harms acoustic and thermal insulation between areas.

renovations.cost_he: A solid built addition: ~$6,000–12,000 per built m² (shell + finish), i.e. a ~15 m² room ≈ $90,000–180,000+. Building onto the roof/an additional story is more expensive due to the reinforcement needed.
renovations.permit_he: A building permit is mandatory — added built area. Requires an architect, an engineer, and sometimes a variance/change to the building plan; a long process.
renovations.duration_he: Construction 2–5 months; but the permit and preliminary design may add 6–18 months.
considerations_he: Depends on the existing building rights for the lot (the building plan/built-area ratio) — check with the authority before anything else.
Connecting to the existing house requires matching the foundations, roof, waterproofing and an energy connection — weak points for moisture.
An addition increases the municipal tax and a possible development levy — budget beyond the construction cost.
In outlying areas, the availability of a shell contractor and supply lead times may stretch the schedule.

renovations.cost_he: A flat roof: ~$80–200 per m² depending on the method (bituminous membranes, acrylic/polyurethane sealing). An ~80 m² roof ≈ $7,000–18,000. A spot repair $1,500–5,000.
renovations.permit_he: Exempt from a permit (maintenance/waterproofing). Solar panels on the roof are also exempt under conditions — check with the authority.
renovations.duration_he: 2–6 days depending on the area and the number of layers; requires dry weather and a clean roof surface.
considerations_he: First locate the source of the moisture — sometimes the problem is in cracks/joints and not the whole roof.
In Larkmont: large day/night temperature swings and strong UV radiation age the waterproofing — choose a UV-resistant system.
Proper drainage and water runoff are as important as the waterproofing itself — standing water destroys any membrane.
Colored/white (cool) sealing reduces the heat load on the roof — an energy advantage in the hot summer climate.

renovations.cost_he: A mini-central system for a house ~$20,000–45,000 including ducts and units; a large/multi-zone central system $40,000–80,000+. A condenser + air handler alone $9,000–20,000.
renovations.permit_he: Generally exempt from a permit. The placement of the condenser unit on the facade/roof — make sure it doesn't violate an approved facade/the bylaws; a dedicated electrical connection is required.
renovations.duration_he: Installation 3–7 days; easiest to do during a general renovation when the ceilings/walls are open for the ducts.
considerations_he: A mini-central system requires acoustic/drywall ceiling space for the ducts — plan it together with the flooring/ceilings.
In Larkmont the load is mainly heating in winter — consider a heat pump/AC that heats efficiently (a high COP).
The placement of the condenser unit affects noise and efficiency — not facing a bedroom window, and not in an air-blocked spot.
Wiring and prep for a smart thermostat is cheap now — zoning planning saves energy over time.

renovations.cost_he: Replacing/upgrading the electrical panel ~$3,000–8,000; adding a point (outlet/light) ~$200–500 per point; full house wiring ~$15,000–40,000 depending on size and complexity.
renovations.permit_he: Exempt from a building permit, but electrical work requires a certified electrician and an inspection by the power utility for the connection/upgrade; compliance with the electrical standards.
renovations.duration_he: Panel replacement 1–2 days; full wiring during a renovation 1–2 weeks (easiest when the walls are exposed).
considerations_he: An old panel without a residual-current device/proper grounding is a safety hazard — upgrading the residual-current devices (RCD) comes first of all.
Capacity suited to a modern load (oven, air conditioners, an electric vehicle, induction) — check the connection size with the power utility.
Add points/outlets generously while the walls are open — adding them later requires breaking into the walls.
Future-proofing: an EV charging station, solar panels, data — empty conduits are cheap now.

renovations.cost_he: Replacing the water/sewage piping for a house ~$15,000–45,000 depending on size and accessibility; a spot job for a bathroom/kitchen $3,000–10,000. Piping in the wall/floor is more expensive than exposed piping.
renovations.permit_he: Exempt from a permit, but a licensed plumber and compliance with the waterproofing and plumbing standard are mandatory; connecting to the municipal line/water meter goes through the authority.
renovations.duration_he: 1–3 weeks for a house; involves breaking into walls/floors and finishing repairs afterward.
considerations_he: Replacing the plumbing is among the costliest and most invasive jobs — it involves breaking in, re-waterproofing and re-tiling.
Most cost-effective to do together with a kitchen/bathroom renovation when the walls and floor are open anyway.
Old piping (iron/lead) is worth replacing with polyethylene/multilayer — for reliability and drinking-water health.
Hot water: consider pipe insulation and proximity to the boiler/solar collectors — significant energy savings in a cold climate.

renovations.cost_he: About $18–40 per m² of wall/ceiling for labor (material included) depending on the wall's condition and the number of coats; a whole apartment/floor ~$4,000–12,000. Skim coating/wall repairs are extra.
renovations.duration_he: 2–5 days for an average apartment including prep, skim coating and drying between coats; moving/covering furniture.
considerations_he: Wall preparation (skim coating, sanding, primer) determines the result more than the paint itself.
Quality washable paint (for children's rooms/the hallway) saves repainting — worth the difference.
In wet rooms/outdoors — anti-mold/exterior acrylic paint is more durable.
The cheapest and most cost-effective of all the renovations in terms of effect-per-cost; easy for partial DIY.

renovations.cost_he: Exterior facade plaster/spray ~$90–250 per m² depending on the method (acrylic skim coat, colored plaster, cladding). A whole private-home facade ~$25,000–70,000+ including scaffolding.
renovations.permit_he: Renewing/painting a facade — usually exempt. But changing the facade's appearance, stone cladding or adding external (thermal) insulation may require approval/a permit — check with the authority and the bylaws.
renovations.duration_he: 1–4 weeks depending on the facade area, including erecting scaffolding, preparation, coats and drying.
considerations_he: Cracks in the facade sometimes originate from a structural/moisture problem — diagnose them before covering them with new plaster.
External thermal insulation (an EIFS-type system) is more expensive but dramatically improves comfort and energy — relevant to Larkmont's extreme climate.
Scaffolding is a significant chunk of the cost — consolidate all the exterior work (waterproofing/paint/windows too) into the same opportunity.
UV-resistant and salt/dust-resistant facade paint really matters here; light shades reduce the heat load.

renovations.cost_he: An aluminum window/door with insulating (double) glazing ~$1,500–4,000 per unit depending on size and profile; a whole house ~$25,000–80,000+. A thermal-break profile and an electric shutter add to the cost.
renovations.permit_he: Replacement in the same opening — usually exempt. Enlarging an opening/changing the facade or creating a new opening — requires a permit and sometimes an engineer (if it touches a load-bearing wall).
renovations.duration_he: 1–4 days for a house depending on the quantity; relatively clean work if the openings keep their size.
considerations_he: Double/insulating glazing dramatically changes comfort and energy — critical in Larkmont's climate (cold nights, strong sun).
The quality of the sealing around the frame (filler, silicone, sill) is as important as the window itself — it prevents the ingress of dust/water/cold.
Shutters/external shading save more than glazing alone in summer — consider a combined shutter/grille.
A 'thermal break' profile prevents condensation/a cold frame in winter — a worthwhile upgrade at the high end.

renovations.name_he: Adding / upgrading a reinforced safe room
renovations.cost_he: Building a new reinforced safe room (~9–12 m², concrete walls, a reinforced door + window, ventilation) ~$70,000–150,000+ depending on size and accessibility. Upgrading/replacing just the reinforced door/window $6,000–20,000.
renovations.permit_he: A separate track — approval under the safe-room standard for the room's specification, plus a building permit from the authority (added area/a structural element). Requires an engineer.
renovations.duration_he: Construction 1–3 months; including design, standard approval and the permit — in total sometimes 6–12 months.
considerations_he: A safe room is not an ordinary room — concrete wall thickness, a reinforced door and window, ventilation/filtration per the safe-room standard; do not improvise.
The location of the safe room affects accessibility in an emergency — close to the living areas, not at the far end of the house.
It can be designed to serve as a bedroom/office in everyday use, but its protective function must not be compromised (an unobstructed door, ventilation).
When added to an existing house — connecting the foundations and the structure requires an engineer; this is a load-bearing element.

renovations.cost_he: Pergola: wood ~$600–1,500 per m², aluminum/metal ~$1,000–2,500 per m². Decking/yard paving ~$250–700 per m². Full yard landscaping (paving, gardening, lighting, irrigation) $30,000–120,000+ depending on the area.
renovations.permit_he: An open pergola (not sealed-roofed) up to certain area/height limits — exempt under the regional exemption rules, subject to conditions. A sealed roof/enclosing a balcony/a solid structure — requires a permit. A fence above a certain height — check.
renovations.duration_he: Pergola 2–7 days; full yard landscaping 2–6 weeks depending on the scope of the gardening, paving and planting.
considerations_he: An open pergola is exempt only if it meets the exemption conditions (shading gaps, size, distance from the boundary) — verify before building.
In Larkmont: shading from the strong sun and wind-breaking matter; UV- and dust-resistant materials.
Combine yard landscaping with planning the irrigation, drainage and lighting — infrastructure is cheap before the paving.
Native/water-thrifty vegetation (xeriscape) suits the dry climate and saves on irrigation and maintenance.
